trace.rationale: Historical MLB data shows roughly 55% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no strong team-specific or situational adjustments available.

Historical data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run in the first inning across MLB games, with slight variation based on team strengths. No specific team or pitcher info provided, so baseline estimate.

Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run scoring in the first inning, with slight variation based on team strengths and starting pitchers. Without specific game details, a neutral estimate near the historical average is appropriate.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run scoring in the first inning, with slight variation based on team strengths and starting pitchers.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 48-50% of first innings have no runs, so the probability of at least one run is slightly above 50%.
Historical MLB data shows roughly 55% of first innings have at least one run scored, with no specific evidence to deviate from this baseline.

Historical data shows that in MLB games, roughly 48-52% of first innings see at least one run scored, with slight variation by team and pitcher quality. Without specific team or pitcher details, the base rate is near 50%, slightly tilted to yes due to the home team advantage and typical scoring patterns.

Historical data shows roughly 50-55% chance of a run in the first inning across MLB games, with slight variation by teams and pitchers. No specific game details provided, so baseline estimate applies.
Historical data shows first-inning runs occur in about 60-70% of MLB games, with no strong team-specific factors altering this baseline.
